786.sourceSolution: Solution: A. Utilize project retrospective sessions for discussions and learning. Project retrospective sessions are a key tool for continuous improvement in agile project management. They provide an opportunity for the team to reflect on the previous sprint, identify areas for improvement, and develop a plan to address those areas in the next sprint. In this case, the project manager can utilize retrospectives to gather ideas from the team on how to enhance productivity in the new agile environment. The project manager can then work with the team to develop and implement a plan to implement these ideas. The other options are incorrect. Effective iteration planning is vital, and iteration planning sessions are not the best forum for discussing and adopting new ways of working. It is meant for planning the work to be completed in the upcoming iteration. Developing and sharing guidelines for team training and mentoring is not the best way to leverage the team's ideas in the immediate term. The product owner is responsible for prioritizing the product backlog and ensuring that the team is working on the most important requirements. It is not the product owner's responsibility to facilitate the transition to agile. Fast-tracking the transition does not guarantee that the team members have learned how best to work in the new agile environment.

799.sourceSolution: Solution: B. Review the project plan to determine whether the missing features were part of the original scope or were added later. The project manager should first review the project plan and scope to determine whether the missing features were part of the original scope or were added later. This will help to identify the reasons for the missing features and develop a plan to address them. If the missing features were part of the original scope, the project manager should collaborate with the team to identify the reasons for the missing features and develop a plan to address them. This may involve revising the project plan, adjusting priorities, and allocating additional resources as necessary. If the missing features are added later, the project manager should work with the client to determine if they are critical to the project's success and if they can be included in a future release. It may also be helpful to communicate the plan to stakeholders and obtain their buy-in. The other options are incorrect. Evaluating the missing features with the client and planning to include the requirements in the next phase is premature. A comprehensive understanding of the missing features is crucial before making plans. Requesting additional funding is premature. The project manager must have a better understanding of the issue before making a justified request for additional funding. Restarting the project is an extreme measure and may not be possible or necessary.

809.sourceSolution: Solution: C. Complete the sprint review and release the MVP with the completed features. The goal of an MVP is to release a product with the core features that are needed to validate the product concept and get feedback from users. The team has completed the must-have and should-have features, which are the most important features for the MVP. Must and Should requirements refer to the MoSCoW (Must Have, Should Have, Could Have, Won't Have this time) practice of prioritizing requirements. If all the "must have" and "should have" requirements are met, the other requirements are not mandatory. Releasing the MVP with the completed features will allow the team to get feedback from users on the core features and make necessary adjustments before developing the other priority features. It also assumes that adding more resources will accelerate completion, which may not always be the case.

excludeReasons.A: Daily coordination meetings are brief collaboration meetings during which the project team reviews its progress from the previous day, declares intentions for the current day, and highlights any obstacles encountered or anticipated.
excludeReasons.B: Lessons learned meetings typically happen at the end of a project and are designed to capture valuable insights and lessons from the project, which can be used to enhance future projects.
excludeReasons.D: The iteration planning meeting is incorrect because that is not the nature of those meetings.

825.sourceSolution: Solution: C. At the next retrospective meeting. Sharing observations and agreeing on adjustments for improvement to the way of working should be done at the retrospective meeting. Retrospective meetings are usually held at the end of each sprint or iteration and provide an opportunity for the project team to review how it works and to suggest changes to improve processes and efficiency. This meeting gives the project manager a chance to discuss with the team and identify if there is a problem with comfort levels regarding agile processes. The other options are incorrect. The iteration planning meeting is incorrect because that is not the nature of those meetings. An iteration planning meeting is held to clarify the acceptance criteria for backlog items and the work effort needed to fulfill an upcoming iteration commitment. Daily coordination meetings are brief collaboration meetings during which the project team reviews its progress from the previous day, declares intentions for the current day, and highlights any obstacles encountered or anticipated. The team is not facing issues because they are delivering on time, but the project manager perceives a comfort level issue. Lessons learned meetings typically happen at the end of a project and are designed to capture valuable insights and lessons from the project, which can be used to enhance future projects. It helps in identifying areas for improvement and recognizing successful practices. Raising it at the next retrospective meeting is preferred over the final lessons learned meeting since the adjusted behavior will increase value in the remaining iterations of the project.
825.whyCorrect: C. At the next retrospective meeting. Sharing observations and agreeing on adjustments for improvement to the way of working should be done at the retrospective meeting. Retrospective meetings are usually held at the end of each sprint or iteration and provide an opportunity for the project team to review how it works and to suggest changes to improve processes and efficiency. This meeting gives the project manager a chance to discuss with the team and identify if there is a problem with comfort levels regarding agile processes.

838.sourceSolution: Solution: B. Collaborate with the team and stakeholders to discuss tailoring the existing governance. The project manager should collaborate with the project team and the senior stakeholder to discuss tailoring the existing governance. This will help ensure that governance is tailored to the specific needs of the new project while still incorporating successful elements from the previous project. Tailoring involves the analysis, design, and deliberate modification of project governance to make it more suitable for the given environment and the work at hand. Collaboration with the stakeholders and the project team is important to ensure that the governance structure is appropriate and effective. The other options are incorrect. Meeting with the PMO could be helpful, but it may be premature to seek their insight before discussing it with the team and stakeholders. Informing stakeholders that the same project governance structure can be used for the new project is premature and may not be accurate or appropriate. The new project may have unique components, the business environment may have shifted, and the team may have identified areas for improvement in the previous project. All these factors should be considered before committing to the same governance structure. Requesting that stakeholders discuss all governance-related concerns with the product owner is not an effective approach, as the full project team should be involved in collaborative governance discussions.

839.sourceSolution: Solution: A. Work with the team to develop a corrective action plan. Sprint reviews and/or retrospectives are special meetings where the team gathers after completing a work increment to inspect and adapt their methods and teamwork. These meetings foster collective learning, act as catalysts for change, and generate concrete actions. Clear benefits have been observed, such as increased productivity, improved capability, enhanced quality, and increased delivery capacity through iterative adjustments based on retrospective outcomes. In addition to tangible benefits, retrospectives also boost team empowerment and enjoyment. Addressing sprint quality issues and team morale in the retrospective is crucial to creating a safe environment for open discussion. The other options are incorrect. It may not be possible to recover time, and without developing a corrective plan, they would not know how to best move forward. Agile values adaptability over rigid planning, prioritizes delivering value, and focuses on continuous improvement. Identifying causes of delays and working together for sustainable solutions is essential for better future sprints. Agile practices stress prioritizing quality over simply increasing team size. Adding members without addressing root causes of problems can reduce efficiency. It's important to analyze issues first and consider improving collaboration or task structure before expanding the team. Instead of simply making demands, it is more effective to create a supportive environment where, during the retrospective, the team can collaboratively identify and address obstacles that affect their velocity. This approach not only promotes continuous improvement of processes and efficiency but also facilitates constructive discussions to ensure that any changes align with the team's agile goals and principles.

Clear benefits have been observed, such as increased productivity, improved capability, enhanced quality, and increased delivery capacity through iterative adjustments based on retrospective outcomes. In addition to tangible benefits, retrospectives also boost team empowerment and enjoyment. Addressing sprint quality issues and team morale in the retrospective is crucial to creating a safe environment for open discussion.

865.sourceSolution: Solution: A. Work with stakeholders to review external competition, assess its effect on launch assumptions, and suggest an adjusted rollout plan. Working with relevant stakeholders in this way explicitly evaluates the external competitive change and uses that analysis to decide whether and how to adapt the launch strategy. The competitor announcement represents a significant external development that may affect the expected benefits and assumptions behind the launch strategy, but it does not automatically dictate a specific response such as an immediate delay or deep discounting. The project manager’s responsibility is to facilitate structured analysis and informed decision-making rather than reacting impulsively. This supports benefits realization and alignment with organizational strategy instead of simply focusing on schedule or reacting to pressure. The other options are incorrect. Proceeding exactly as planned, without revisiting assumptions or assessing market impact, treats the plan as fixed regardless of changing conditions and undermines effective benefits management. Immediately postponing the launch by several months and committing to a comprehensive redesign is an extreme response that is not supported by analysis. It delays time-to-market without first confirming whether a redesign is necessary or feasible. Cutting the price by 50% to match or beat the competitor’s promotion, without analysis, is a reactive decision that can erode margins, harm long-term pricing strategy, and may still not address other factors such as app quality, features, or brand positioning

872.sourceSolution: Solution: A. Launch the product in Region X and plan future iterations to address noncompliance in other regions. An agile approach refines work items and delivers them frequently. It also ensures quality control to comply with requirements throughout the project. In the case of non-compliance, the project manager should plan iterations to implement other requirements and standards. The project manager should prioritize ensuring that the product meets the standards and requirements of the region where it will be launched first. Once the product is launched in Region X, the project manager can then plan future iterations to meet the standards and requirements of the other regions. This approach enables the project manager to address the competition threat while ensuring that the product meets the needs of all target markets. The other options are incorrect. Documenting this as noncompliance in the quality management plan does not address the fact that the product fails to meet the standards and requirements of two other regions. Pausing the launch in Region X delays the launch and ignores the competitive threat. In this instance, it is important to launch the product in Region X as a baseline in response to a competitive threat. Pausing to replan all iterations to meet the standards of all three regions within the scope of the work would require a significant amount of rework and could significantly delay the product's launch. 873.sourceSolution: Solution: B. $45,000 EMV gives the average cost you would expect if this same uncertain risk situation occurred many times. It is used to help determine contingency reserves by aggregating the EMVs of multiple risks. Expected monetary value (EMV) for a single risk is calculated as: EMV = Probability × Impact. From the scenario: Probability = 30% = 0.30 Impact (cost if it occurs) = $150,000 So: EMV = 0.30 × 150,000 = $45,000 Therefore, the correct option is $45,000. The other options are inaccurate due to errors in calculation.

882.sourceSolution: Solution: D. Meet with the team member to understand their selection behavior and tailor the workload accordingly. In agile environments, addressing team performance issues effectively requires understanding the root cause of behaviors. By meeting one-on-one with the team member, the project manager can identify potential reasons, such as confidence issues, skill gaps, or workload preferences, and adjust the approach to better support both the individual and the team. This tailored, empathetic interaction aligns with agile leadership, fostering collaboration and continuous improvement. The other options are incorrect. Reminding the entire team to prioritize high-value stories may overlook personal challenges and does not address individual behavior specifically. Instructing the team member to select different stories is a directive approach that may reduce motivation and disempower the team member, conflicting with agile leadership principles. This focuses on mandating responsibilities and fails to address the root cause of the issue. Providing technical training is only effective if a skills gap is identified as the cause of the issue. Without that knowledge, it may be unnecessary and inefficient.

885.sourceSolution: Solution: D. Identify applicable compliance requirements early and integrate them into project plans and activities. By identifying applicable requirements early and integrating them into the project plans and activities, the project manager ensures that compliance is treated as a core part of scope and design rather than an afterthought. This means incorporating compliance tasks into the work breakdown structure, including relevant milestones and reviews in the schedule, and engaging compliance, security, and sustainability experts as part of the project team or as key stakeholders. It also supports risk management, because many of these requirements directly relate to legal, operational, and reputational risks that must be mitigated during planning and execution. The other answer choices are incorrect. This option ignores the integrative role of the project manager and fails to embed compliance into the project’s baseline.

902.sourceSolution: Solution: D. Apply lessons learned during execution by incorporating agreed improvements into current ways of working. By actively applying lessons learned during execution, the project manager closes the loop between learning and action. Retrospectives move from being purely reflective sessions to becoming mechanisms for real-time adaptation. This creates an environment of iterative improvement, where each cycle of work benefits from what was learned in the previous one, leading to better quality, efficiency, and stakeholder satisfaction during the current project, not just in future projects. This approach also increases transparency for the sponsor and other stakeholders. The other answer choices are incorrect. While maintaining a log creates some structure for capturing insights, delaying their application to future projects means the current project gains little or no benefit from what is being learned now. This approach still treats lessons learned as something to be used later rather than a mechanism for immediate adaptation. When improvement ideas are shared informally, and implementation is left to individuals, changes are adopted inconsistently, reducing the likelihood of systematic application or visible project-level benefits. It lacks deliberate, agreed, and trackable changes in work methods, so sponsors and stakeholders may not see clear continuous improvement throughout the project.

931.sourceSolution: Solution: B. Integrate the plan-do-check-act (PDCA) cycle into workflows to embed continuous project improvement. A feedback-rich culture with frequent data-based information communicated regularly helps to standardize improvements and ensure effective updates for successful project outcomes. The PDCA cycle institutionalizes improvement and matches the sponsor’s request. Plan: Identify weaknesses and create improvement plans. Do: Test solutions and implement changes. Check: Monitor and evaluate the impact of the changes by analyzing performance data. Act: Sustain improvements and standardize successful practices. This approach is dynamic and embeds sustained improvement directly into workflows, making it the ideal choice for achieving continuous improvement as directed by the sponsor. The other options are incorrect. While using SMART objectives is important for setting clear benchmarks, this option is focused on defining measurable goals rather than the actual process of achieving continuous improvement. Benchmarking is a useful tool, but it focuses more on external comparisons rather than using internal performance data collected from the project. Root-cause analysis solves issues reactively; it is not a sustained framework. Root-cause analysis is a great tool to identify the underlying causes of specific problems, but it is reactive (used after deviations recur) rather than proactive.

938.sourceSolution: Solution: AI can support project management tasks in many ways. The more complex the task, the more human intervention is required to result in high-quality outcomes. In that sense, a good strategy is classifying tasks according to complexity and the need for human supervision. As a result of this classification, the opportunities to use AI fall into three categories: Automation Tasks that are low complexity and require little human intervention in their final output can be automated. Common examples are report generation, document analysis, and conference call summarization. Standard prompts can be created and reused across different projects and teams. A good source of those prompts is the PMI Prompt Library in the PMI Infinity™ tool. Assistance At the assistance level, the tools complement analysis and iteratively build ideas toward an expected output. The result of the first iterations should not be considered complete without further analysis and refinement. Some examples include creating a risk register or a scheduling plan with buffers. A project professional is expected to review the results to ensure they are accurate and complete. Augmentation Augmentation is the enhancement of existing capabilities and the exploration of new ones by professionals. Augmentation focuses on strategic and complex tasks, such as balancing the options in a project portfolio to maximize return on investment or performing risk forecasting based on external variables. Project professionals should use the tool as a brainstorming partner, exchanging ideas and refining the results through multiple iterations.

943.sourceSolution: Solution: A. Examine the organizational culture to align the selection with business outcomes and identify the values that drive organizational change. By taking a values-based approach and exploring how the available offerings align with business outcomes and organizational culture, the project team can ensure that the solution they select is best suited to the specific company and is not just selected based on their understanding of the industry or other less-tailored metrics. The other options are incorrect. An adaptive approach does not fit the contractual requirements of selecting a new technology solution. Most technology products require contracts that cannot easily be adjusted after they are signed. It might be possible to have a trial period and make adjustments based on findings, but without a clear understanding of the organizational culture, it would be difficult to accurately analyze the product’s alignment with organizational objectives and values. Neither a cost analysis nor stakeholder feedback will identify the organizational culture and values on their own. Financial priorities and stakeholder requirements are both important factors in making a final decision, but they do not inherently reveal the organizational values that drive metrics such as adoption, usage, and satisfaction. Without analyzing the desired business outcomes, values, and culture, there is no guarantee that the selected solution will deliver maximum benefit to the overall company.

963.sourceSolution: Solution: A, B A. Methodology for identifying, analyzing, responding, and monitoring risks. B. Roles and responsibilities assigned to the risk management activities. Methodology involves defining how risks will be identified, analyzed (qualitative and/or quantitative methods), responded to, and monitored. A clear methodology ensures consistency and systematic execution of risk management activities and is an essential part of the risk management plan. Assigning roles and responsibilities for risk management activities ensures accountability and clarity regarding who will manage specific risks. The other options are incorrect. Forecasting tools for tracking project performance focus on project monitoring and control rather than on specific risk management. While forecasting tools may help assess performance trends, they are not inherently part of a risk management plan's core components. Configuration management pertains to controlling changes to deliverables and project scope, but it is not directly related to how risks will be managed. This belongs to a different aspect of project management. The risk management plan does not explicitly outline corrective actions. Corrective actions would be part of the risk response plan, which is developed later when actual risks have been identified. The risk management plan itself defines the framework and approach, not individual responses.
963.whyCorrect: A, B A. Methodology for identifying, analyzing, responding, and monitoring risks. B. Roles and responsibilities assigned to the risk management activities. A clear methodology ensures consistency and systematic execution of risk management activities and is an essential part of the risk management plan. Assigning roles and responsibilities for risk management activities ensures accountability and clarity regarding who will manage specific risks.

985.sourceSolution: Solution: B. Execute a change request and submit it to the change control board so the impact of the change can be reviewed before acceptance or rejection. In predictive projects with strict oversight or guidelines, such as those involving government contracts, proposed changes must be thoroughly reviewed to analyze the impacts and risks related to approving or rejecting the request to minimize negative outcomes. A change control board is a formally established group responsible for reviewing, evaluating, approving, deferring, or rejecting changes, as well as documenting and communicating these changes to the stakeholders and project team. The other options are less effective. A verbal discussion does not guarantee adequate review or documentation of the requested changes. Although changes can be initiated verbally, they should be documented in writing and entered into the change or configuration management system. Change requests should also include information on the estimated schedule and cost impacts before they can be approved. Backlog management processes are exclusive to adaptive approaches, which would not be appropriate for a project of this nature. Adaptive approaches utilize a continuous assessment and prioritization of necessary changes throughout the development process through the use of a backlog management process. For projects that involve strict oversight, such as government contracts, a more formal change request and review process is necessary. Meeting with stakeholders does not constitute a formal change request. Although stakeholders can initiate a change request, neither they nor the project team is responsible for reviewing or approving such requests. In a predictive environment, all change requests should be reviewed by an authoritative body, such as the project manager, project sponsor, or change control board.

990.sourceSolution: Solution: B. Facilitate a team planning session to strategize responses for all prioritized risks, starting with the highest risks. Higher-priority risks, such as Risk 1, typically have the greatest potential to negatively impact project objectives. Addressing these first ensures that the project team focuses its efforts on mitigating or managing the most substantial threats, protecting the program's strategic outcomes. This will help determine whether a risk should be escalated based on its impact and scope, or handled within the team’s authority. The team should present the findings, backed by the risk register, exposure levels, and impact assessments, to the program sponsor for discussion and evidence-based recommendations. The other options are incorrect. Escalation would transfer ownership to senior management and should only occur if the risk exceeds project authority. This should not be considered if mitigation could address the issue at the project level. Risk response strategies should be developed during the risk planning process, not after a risk materializes. Taking action only if the risk materializes is a reactive approach. The project manager should not take a "wait and see” approach to prioritized risks. Deflecting the sponsor’s concern undermines trust and does not address risk exposure.

1023.sourceSolution: Solution: C. Implement iterative feedback mechanisms to allow readjustments to real-time external inputs and ensure delivered value remains high. In industries characterized by high dynamism, such as financial technology, projects must be managed flexibly to adjust to rapidly evolving external factors. Incorporating iterative feedback mechanisms allows scope adjustments to real-time external inputs and enables the project team to continuously refine and realign the project’s objectives to maintain relevance and ensure the delivered value remains high. The other options do not guarantee flexibility or ensure the project team can adapt quickly to changing market conditions. Although it can be useful to have a variety of user stories in the backlog to work with, no project team can predict every technological advance or market fluctuation that occurs throughout the course of the project. Without an iterative approach in place to react to such hurdles, having a large variety of inputs will not be beneficial to adapting to changing market conditions. Scheduling buffers and reserves into the project management plan can give the project team more space in which to react to real-time external inputs, but this does not guarantee flexibility on its own. If the project team does not use that time to iterate and adapt the project scope, it is likely that the project’s delivery schedule will still be negatively impacted. Organizing a contingency reserve in the project budget will be useful only if the project team has correctly assessed the risks and challenges that may arise and has an effective plan in place to react to them accordingly. Unexpected developments are not typically covered in the contingency reserve, and without iterative feedback mechanisms, there is no guarantee that the project team can react quickly and efficiently to the new inputs.

1029.sourceSolution: Solution: B. Present continuation options framed by aggregated risk exposure and remaining response flexibility. The project manager must present an integrated, forward-looking view of risk, showing how the combination of medium risks increases overall exposure and how much flexibility remains to respond, so that leadership can make an informed continuation decision under regulatory and contractual constraints. Framing options around aggregated exposure and remaining response flexibility aligns with risk-based decision-making and respects formal governance requirements in a regulated, multi-supplier environment. The other options are incorrect. Approving continuation simply because no single risk exceeds its threshold overlooks the combined impact of several rising medium risks, which can lead to significant overall exposure. This approach may leave leadership underinformed when making key decisions that could restrict future options. Waiting to discuss risks only after they escalate is reactive, limits the chance to influence decisions early, and does not meet leadership’s expectation for recommendations based on current risk trends.

1033.sourceSolution: Solution: B. Report the compliance issue, update on remediation, and outline governance choices affecting delivery. The issue is a known compliance weakness in a high-visibility project that will not be fully resolved before a key governance review, and external auditors may later request evidence. The project manager must therefore disclose the compliance risk transparently in formal governance materials, explain the remediation status, and present clear governance choices so that decision makers can consciously accept, mitigate, or avoid the risk in a documented way. The other options are incorrect. 1041.sourceSolution: Solution: D. Submit a formal change request to adjust the baseline budget and timelines using updated estimates. The scenario explicitly says variances exceed the thresholds defined in the governance plan. That means the project manager should analyze the causes and impacts, update the forecast using current information, and then submit a formal change request if the budget and schedule baselines need to change. This preserves governance integrity, ensures sponsor visibility, and creates a documented path for approved rebaselining. The other options are incorrect. Negotiating with suppliers and trying to accelerate permits may be valid corrective actions. Still, once cost and schedule variances exceed escalation thresholds, the project manager should not update the baselines directly. Increased monitoring, reporting, and planning may be helpful, but they do not address the key trigger in the scenario: the variances have already exceeded the escalation thresholds. At that point, monitoring alone is not enough. The project manager must move the issue through formal governance and determine whether a baseline change requires approval. Tighter procurement oversight and resource optimization may help reduce future variance, and they may be part of the recovery plan. However, this option treats the issue mainly as an operational control problem. It does not address the fact that the current variances have already crossed escalation limits and therefore require formal review and likely change control.

1049.sourceSolution: Solution: D. Ask the product owner to add the enhancement requests to the product backlog. In agile projects, the backlog is progressively elaborated and reprioritized to identify the work that can be accomplished in an upcoming iteration. Unplanned requests for enhancements should be properly evaluated and prioritized by the product owner. This ensures that the team can focus on the committed work for the current iteration and reduces the risk of missing scheduled tasks. The other options are incorrect. 1055.sourceSolution: Solution: C. Track team satisfaction and engagement scores to identify early signs of delivery challenges. In agile projects, the performance and motivation of the collaborative, cross-functional team are critical for success. If team satisfaction and engagement decrease, it could indicate underlying problems such as frustration, burnout, unclear goals, resource constraints, or conflicts. These issues may lead to delayed delivery or suboptimal outcomes. Tracking team engagement is innovative because it acts as a leading indicator, a metric that predicts potential delivery risks before they occur. Monitoring satisfaction helps identify challenges early that could affect delivery timelines or quality. The other options are incorrect. Customer satisfaction is a reactive indicator that surfaces issues only after delivery or release. Defect density is a lagging indicator that surfaces issues only after delivery or release. Negative social media reviews are lagging measures that surface issues only after delivery or release.
1055.whyCorrect: C. Track team satisfaction and engagement scores to identify early signs of delivery challenges. In agile projects, the performance and motivation of the collaborative, cross-functional team are critical for success. If team satisfaction and engagement decrease, it could indicate underlying problems such as frustration, burnout, unclear goals, resource constraints, or conflicts. These issues may lead to delayed delivery or suboptimal outcomes. Tracking team engagement is innovative because it acts as a leading indicator, a metric that predicts potential delivery risks before they occur. Monitoring satisfaction helps identify challenges early that could affect delivery timelines or quality.

1075.sourceSolution: Solution: B. Assess each project risk for its probability of occurrence and impact. Qualitative risk analysis evaluates individual project risks for their probability of occurrence and impact. It can include other characteristics such as degree of impact, manageability, timing, relationships with other risks, and common causes or effects. The assessment of risks for these characteristics at the beginning of the project provides insights that will inform risk management throughout the project. The other options are incorrect. One task in risk management planning is to identify potential risk owners and record them in the risk register. However, this step is more of a help with managing risks throughout the project than with initially analyzing the nature and impact of the risks. Simulation models provide valuable insights into how risks relate to each other, which is useful for risk management, but they do not address the nature of individual risks as a risk analysis does. Root cause analysis helps determine the underlying cause of each risk and is an important tool for the project manager to use when needed. However, the initial stage of risk analysis serves a different purpose: providing a total picture of the nature and impact of the risks rather than a deep investigation into the cause of a particular risk.

1113.sourceSolution: Solution: C. Discuss the changes with the product owner and assess the impact of making changes late in a sprint. The first step that the project manager should take is to discuss and assess the implications of making changes during an iteration with the product owner. When scope changes arise late in a sprint, they can impact overall project timelines and budget, which the product owner may not be aware of. It's important for the project manager and product owner to evaluate whether changes made late in a sprint are critical enough to justify delivery delays because this decision impacts project delivery. The other options are incorrect. Escalating the issue to senior management should be a last resort. It is important for the project manager to try to understand and address the issue directly with the product owner first. Informing the team to ignore the changes requested by the product owner is not a suitable solution, as it can lead to conflict between the project manager, the product owner, and the team. While agile approaches respond to change, it is important to ensure that changes are necessary, provide value, and that impacts are understood. The changes will impact delivery timelines, so expecting the team to deliver on time despite the changes is not reasonable.

1121.signalAnswer: Stem signals (scenario cues) → C: Submit the customer's change request for review following formal change control procedures.
whyBullets: C is correct (reference solution): C. Submit the customer's change request for review following formal change control procedures. Any request to alter the project budget after completion, even when the project is under budget or has unused contingency reserves, must be formally submitted and reviewed through integ…

Any request to alter the project budget after completion, even when the project is under budget or has unused contingency reserves, must be formally submitted and reviewed through integrated change control procedures.

excludeReasons.A: Closing financials and rejecting the request outright disregards proper evaluation and might negatively impact stakeholder relationships.
excludeReasons.B: Approving the budget change assumes approval before formal review, which is inappropriate.
excludeReasons.D: Recording the request in the lessons learned register and informing the customer of project closure archives the request, but does not follow the formal change control process required for budget modifications.

1121.sourceSolution: Solution: C. Submit the customer's change request for review following formal change control procedures. Any request to alter the project budget after completion, even when the project is under budget or has unused contingency reserves, must be formally submitted and reviewed through integrated change control procedures. This involves a change control board (CCB) that reviews, evaluates, approves, defers, or rejects changes to the project. It ensures that all budget changes are evaluated based on deliverables, project documents, and the project management plan. This approach supports the transparency and governance essential for maintaining the integrity and alignment of the project's objectives and plans. The other options are incorrect. Closing financials and rejecting the request outright disregards proper evaluation and might negatively impact stakeholder relationships. Approving the budget change assumes approval before formal review, which is inappropriate. All changes require review from the CCB and explicit approval before implementation. Recording the request in the lessons learned register and informing the customer of project closure archives the request, but does not follow the formal change control process required for budget modifications. This course of action emphasizes recording the request and does not proactively address it.
1121.whyCorrect: C. Submit the customer's change request for review following formal change control procedures. Any request to alter the project budget after completion, even when the project is under budget or has unused contingency reserves, must be formally submitted and reviewed through integrated change control procedures. This involves a change control board (CCB) that reviews, evaluates, approves, defers, or rejects changes to the project. It ensures that all budget changes are evaluated based on deliverables, project documents, and the project management plan. This approach supports the transparency and governance essential for maintaining the integrity and alignment of the project's objectives and plans.

1122.sourceSolution: Solution: B. Perform a quality audit to evaluate adherence to defined processes and identify opportunities for improvement. A quality audit reflects the execution of the quality management plan through quality assurance, which focuses on evaluating whether project activities comply with organizational and project-specific quality standards. A quality audit is the appropriate tool to assess process alignment and uncover improvement opportunities. It is performed during execution and targets how work is being done, not what is being produced. The other options are incorrect. Although inspections and sampling are valid techniques, they are used to assess outputs, not the underlying processes. In this scenario, the concern is about whether work processes still align with expectations; this calls for assurance, not control. Integrating new assurance tools and techniques into the plan is a planning activity, not an execution-phase response. Although integrating new tools may seem responsive to stakeholder concerns, it implies revisiting the planning process rather than evaluating current execution. The scenario calls for assessing whether existing processes are being followed, not redesigning the plan. Escalating unresolved concerns reflects issue management, not quality assurance. Escalation might be necessary if problems persist, but the scenario calls for evaluating process adherence — not resolving stakeholder conflict. The appropriate first step is to assess whether the processes are aligned, not to escalate concerns without investigation.
1122.whyCorrect: B. Perform a quality audit to evaluate adherence to defined processes and identify opportunities for improvement. A quality audit reflects the execution of the quality management plan through quality assurance, which focuses on evaluating whether project activities comply with organizational and project-specific quality standards. A quality audit is the appropriate tool to assess process alignment and uncover improvement opportunities. It is performed during execution and targets how work is being done, not what is being produced.
